did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), a deep learning algorithm, for the classification of skin diseases (Melanoma, Nevus, and Atypical) using the ISIC dataset. It also employs traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, FFNN, KNN) for comparative evaluation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document explicitly labels itself as a 'Research paper' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the design of a unique skin disease identification system using adaptive filtering, region growing segmentation, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The paper details a specific methodology, uses the ISIC dataset for simulation and evaluation, and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 96.768% accuracy) comparing the proposed method against other classifiers.

title: Robust skin diseases detection and classification using deep neural networks
brief_summary: This study proposes a robust Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for detecting and classifying three skin diseases: Melanoma, Nevus, and Atypical. The methodology employs adaptive filtering for pre-processing, an adaptive region growing technique for segmentation, and a hybrid feature extraction method combining 2D-DWT, geometric, and texture features. Classification is performed using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), which are compared against SVM, KNN, ANN, and FFNN classifiers. The experimental analysis utilizes the ISIC1 dataset, with data split 80-20 for training and testing. The proposed CNN-based method achieved a classification accuracy of 96.768%, outperforming other classifiers and state-of-the-art techniques.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin diseases (Melanoma, Nevus, and Atypical)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is the 'classification of skin diseases' for three specific types: Melanoma, Nevus, and Atypical. The model predicts discrete class labels using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and compares it against SVM, KNN, ANN, and FFNN. Performance is measured using accuracy (96.768%), sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC1 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset from the ISIC project containing lesion diagnosed images. The paper lists specific lesion types available in the full dataset (e.g., Actinic keratosis, Fibrous papule, Angioma, Basal cell carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Lentigo NOS, Lentigo simplex 1, Lichenoid keratosis, Seborrheic keratosis, Solar lentigo, Squamous cell carcinoma) but specifies that only three classes were used for the experiments.

use.training: Used for training the classifiers (SVM, KNN, ANN, FFNN, CNN) with 80% of the selected samples.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate set; the paper mentions an 80-20 split for training and testing.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifiers with 20% of the selected samples.
use.other: The dataset also contained 100 normal skin images which were collected but not explicitly included in the classification counts for the three target diseases.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between classifiers (CNN, SVM, KNN, ANN, FFNN) were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC1.dataset_description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset containing 811 lesion images (11 Atypical, 400 Melanoma, 400 Nevus) and 100 normal skin images. Split 80-20% for training and testing.

Disease Classification.description: Classification of three skin diseases: Melanoma, Nevus, and Atypical.
Disease Classification.notes: The CNN classifier showed the highest accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity compared to other classifiers. The approximate classification performance for CNN is noted as 97%.

Disease Detection.description: Detection of whether a test skin image has one of the three diseases or not.
Disease Detection.notes: The proposed method using CNN classifier showed improved results regarding accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.

comparison_with_previous_works.description: Comparison of the proposed method with state-of-art techniques from literature.

comparison_with_previous_works.notes: The proposed method achieved approximately 97% accuracy, which is higher than the compared state-of-art methods listed in Table 4.

architecture_details.description: A deep learning system used for disease classification from extracted features. The paper references Figure 3 showing 'Working of CNN with Many Convolutional Layers' and cites [23] (MathWorks deep learning models) and [24] (ISIC archive) for CNN architecture details, but does not specify the exact layer count, filter sizes, or specific CNN variant (e.g., VGG, ResNet) in the text.
architecture_details.feature_input: The CNN classifier operates on a hybrid feature vector V extracted from the segmented ROI, not raw pixels. The feature vector V consists of: F1 (GLCM Contrast), F2 (GLCM Correlation), F3 (GLCM Energy), F4 (GLCM Homogeneity), F5 (Gradient), F6 (2D-DWT), and F7 (Color features).

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing of the input RGB image
Adaptive noise cancellation using Gaussian filtering
Adaptive noise cancellation using Average filtering
Subtraction of Gaussian and Average filtered images to suppress noisy parts and enhance disease regions
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines adaptive filtering, adaptive region growing segmentation, and a hybrid feature extraction method (2D-DWT, geometric, and texture features) with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier, achieved a classification accuracy of 96.768% on the ISIC1 dataset.
The CNN-based approach outperformed other tested classifiers (SVM, KNN, ANN, and FFNN) in terms of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity for both disease classification and disease detection tasks.

For disease classification, the ARGS + CNN model achieved 96.768% accuracy, 97.449% specificity, and 97.09% sensitivity, while for disease detection, it achieved 96.89% accuracy, 98.11% specificity, and 97.5% sensitivity.
The proposed method demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques reported in previous literature, achieving approximately 97% accuracy.

The adaptive region growing segmentation method effectively localized and extracted the region of interest (ROI) for different skin diseases, contributing to the overall efficiency of the detection system.

The study confirms the effectiveness of using deep learning (CNN) for robust skin disease detection and classification, addressing the challenges of manual feature extraction and improving recognition results over traditional machine learning classifiers.
important_remarks: Severe class imbalance exists in the dataset, with only 11 images for 'Atypical melanocytic proliferation' compared to 400 for Melanoma and Nevus, which may bias the model and limit generalizability for the minority class.

The study relies on a very small total sample size (811 lesion images) split 80-20 for training and testing, which increases the risk of overfitting and limits the statistical robustness of the reported ~97% accuracy.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the ISIC1 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the CNN classifier over other methods, relying only on descriptive metrics.

The CNN architecture details are not fully specified in the text; it is described as operating on hybrid features (GLCM, 2D-DWT, etc.) rather than raw pixels, but specific layer configurations, filter sizes, or hyperparameters are missing.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods in Table 4 is heterogeneous, comparing against techniques targeting different diseases or using different datasets, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid.

Data leakage risk is present as the paper does not explicitly describe how the 80-20 split was performed (e.g., patient-level vs. image-level), which is critical to prevent leakage in medical imaging studies.
The preprocessing steps (adaptive filtering, region growing) are proprietary to this study and not standardized, which may hinder reproducibility and comparison with other CAD systems.
